result:
I've been thinking about the strange beauty of temporary things. A soap bubble exists for maybe thirty seconds, catching light and warping reflections before it pops into nothing. Yet in those moments, it contains whole rainbow worlds on its surface.

There's something deeply human about our attraction to ephemeral experiences. We'll wake up at 4 AM to watch a sunrise, knowing it will last only minutes. We'll travel thousands of miles to see cherry blossoms that bloom for just two weeks. We cherish conversations that evaporate as soon as they're spoken, leaving only the faintest emotional residue.

Maybe it's the scarcity that makes these moments precious. Or perhaps it's because temporary things mirror something true about existence itself—that everything is always in the process of becoming something else. The mountain is becoming sand. The star is becoming iron. The memory is becoming story.

I wonder if permanence would actually diminish beauty rather than preserve it. If sunsets lasted forever, would we still stop to watch them? If flowers never wilted, would we still lean in to smell them? The ticking clock seems to be what makes us pay attention.

Even these words are temporary, existing only for the brief moment you're reading them before your mind moves on to something else. And somehow, that feels exactly right. Not sad, just true. Like a soap bubble that knows it's meant to pop, carrying its small rainbow bravely while it can.
